paragraphs.text: Directorate-General for Justice and Consumers European Commission 1049 Bruxelles/Brussel Belgium
24 October 2025
Re: Digital Fairness Act public consultation
Contributors (in alphabetical order):

Laura Aade (PhD researcher, University of Luxembourg) Dr. Taylor Annabell (postdoctoral researcher, Utrecht University) Dr. Thales Bertaglia (postdoctoral researcher, Utrecht University) Dr. Catalina Goanta (associate professor, Utrecht University) Silviu Istrate (content creator) Jacob van de Kerkhof (doctoral researcher, Utrecht University) Marijn Sax (assistant professor, University of Amsterdam)
To whom it may concern,

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulation manifests itself on online platforms in increasingly diffuse ways
the governance of commercial and political advertising on social media.

paragraphs.text: This is a response to the solicitation for public comments of the European Commission, relating to the Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Our feedback reflects insights from a Romanian content creator and two research projects hosted in the Netherlands: (i) a VENI research project on the ethics and regulation of online manipulation, funded by the Dutch Research Council and hosted at the University of Amsterdam, which analyses the new ways in which manipulation manifests itself on online platforms in increasingly diffuse ways and explores how European legislation can address these forms of manipulation; and (ii) the European Research Council (ERC) Starting Grant HUMANads hosted at Utrecht University, bringing together multidisciplinary insights from law, media studies and computer science around on fairness in the creator economy, particularly with respect to the governance of commercial and political advertising on social media.

Our research is also grounded in the culture and commercial practices in content creation, which is why we have held a focus group with prosocial Romanian content creators in Bucharest on 11 August 2025 (Silviu Faiar, Sabina Teodora, Zlavog, Really Rux, Bushu, Deni, Anca Broasca), and some of the insights and solutions proposed by content creators are mentioned in this answer to the public consultation. Our feedback also incorporates advice we shared with the Council of Europe Committee of Ministers and national rapporteurs on their draft recommendation on online safety and empowerment of content creators and users.

evidence_quotes.quote: DFA's coverage of influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: We welcome the follow-up to the Fairness Check and the DFA's coverage of influencer marketing, which reflects a growing market with increasingly complex business models. We believe the DFA should reflect the much needed attention we ought to give to the fundamental shift in social media platforms, turning from social networks into super-apps with an ever-growing menu of monetization options and platform affordances to facilitate them. New monetization products are regularly rolled-out by social media platforms, in an opaque manner and with often detrimental effects for consumers, such as the lack of transparency. Such new products have led to a highly complex web of platform rules: in our research, we have identified that for TikTok alone, up to 85 platform documents (e.g. Terms of Service, Guidelines and Policies) can be relevant for content creators worldwide (Annabell et al. 2025). This shows a massive shift in how social media is currently used, and where the creator economy is thriving. In our research, we call this shift the New Social Media (van de Kerkhof & Goanta 2024), and it brings together the three most popular activities on the internet: social networking, entertainment, and shopping; or as TikTok calls it, 'shoppertainment' (TikTok 2025).

evidence_quotes.quote: direct consumer payments like subscriptions
an undisclosed influencer marketing campaign
paid political content by influencers

paragraphs.text: We strongly urge the Commission to consider the DFA as a way to holistically address potential issues arising out of the content creation economy. Through platform monetization programmes such as ad-sharing revenue or creator funds, as well as building revenue streams that depend on direct consumer payments like subscriptions, donations and micro-payments of 'gifts', more content is leveraged for commercial purposes than just advertising content. As the Romanian Elections demonstrated, platform monetization including an undisclosed influencer marketing campaign and virtual gifting due livestreams on TikTok is being leveraged for political purposes, intensifying issues of transparency within the context of electoral and civic sphere. As Meta and Google following EU political advertising regulation withdraw from political platform ads, the urgency around how to regulate and enforce the production of paid political content by influencers only increases.

evidence_quotes.quote: For creators with commercial intent, all content production contributes to their self-branding and community-building efforts, generating value that may then be exchanged for payment.

paragraphs.text: For creators with commercial intent, all content production contributes to their self-branding and community-building efforts, generating value that may then be exchanged for payment. This means that creators form a gray area category of natural persons switching between commercial actor and private person, which requires us to rethink how they map onto existing fundamental rights instruments.

evidence_quotes.quote: gambling,

paragraphs.text: Prosocial content creators who put their audiences safety and rights first agree that products and services causing known harms to consumers (e.g. tobacco, gambling,
alcohol, cosmetic surgery) should be rigorously regulated, if not banned. In situations where harms are not well-known, product safety risks should be clarified by brands.

evidence_quotes.quote: contractually demanding legal compliance (e.g. the disclosure of advertising)

paragraphs.text: Information duties and compliance with consumer protection should not solely rest on influencers. Very often, large brands have considerably more legal knowledge, which they can use to educate the influencers they work with by contractually demanding legal compliance (e.g. the disclosure of advertising). Brands and agency intermediaries, including influencer discovery platforms, should also have clear accountability for non-compliance.

section_path: The role of talent agencies and brands

evidence_quotes.quote: Transparency around who is an influencer should first stem from clear criteria applicable to influencers.

paragraphs.text: Transparency around who is an influencer should first stem from clear criteria applicable to influencers. The use of numbers (e.g. followers, reach, or earnings) should be relative to platforms or GDP per capita. One option to implement definitions in practice effectively (regardless of the definition) is to demand platforms to make available influencer account types (see Image 1), and to ask influencers to use such account typologies upon starting to earn systematic revenue out of content creation. Identifying influencers at account level can help consumers distinguish between professional content creators (who systematically monetize their activity) and amateur content creators (who do not yet systematically monetize their activity).

evidence_quotes.quote: distinguishing influencers as distinct category of user

paragraphs.text: Mixed approach from platforms to distinguishing influencers as distinct category of user and verifying accounts as authentic
Image 1 - Overview of account types for influencers/content creators across five platforms

evidence_quotes.quote: native context of influencer marketing
influencers to post stories for a certain period of time to communicate to their audiences their legal infringements

paragraphs.text: Setting legal standards also requires effective enforcement for the native context of influencer marketing, which might require expanding the menu of enforcement actions/sanctions. Injunctions, such as those used by the French Consumer Authority (DGCCRF) who demands influencers to post stories for a certain period of time to communicate to their audiences their legal infringements, could amount to a 'naming and shaming' strategy that may deter some influencers from future infringements. However, it may also have unwanted effects, such as sanctioned influencers becoming the target of increased hate speech, particularly women.

evidence_quotes.quote: gamification of micropayments on livestreaming platforms often leads to exhortations towards children to make monetary donations.
However, not all platforms allow access to audience demographics, and very often, platform demographics only include information about users 18+.
This leaves the platforms in the sole position to identify children, and make sure content moderation is more stringent to avoid unfair commercial practices or media law violations.

paragraphs.text: There is an overlap with Article 28 DSA relating to child safety online. A lot of the creator business models may need to consider minors as vulnerable consumers. For instance, the gamification of micropayments on livestreaming platforms often leads to exhortations towards children to make monetary donations. This is already explicitly prohibited by the UCPD. However, not all platforms allow access to audience demographics, and very often, platform demographics only include information about users 18+. This leaves the platforms in the sole position to identify children, and make sure content moderation is more stringent to avoid unfair commercial practices or media law violations. Channels or users who target children directly or indirectly should not be able to have access to advertising-related monetization affordances. This should be specifically acknowledged as a complementary role of the DSA.

section_path: OVERLAPS WITH OTHER EU REGULATORY REGIMES

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer marketing has been specifically left out of the DSA by narrowing the definition of advertisements (Article 3(r) DSA).

On the one hand, platforms use misleading labels for advertising, which have shaped a wrong public perception that certain business models (e.g. barter, affiliate marketing) should not be disclosed because the payment is not made in money (see Image 2).

paragraphs.text: Influencer marketing has been specifically left out of the DSA by narrowing the definition of advertisements (Article 3(r) DSA). As a result, the transparency obligations for ad repositories do not apply. However, Article 26(2) DSA does ask platforms to offer users functionalities to disclose 'commercial communication', which inevitably will cover influencer marketing (Annabell 2025). This leads to a contradiction, because while not embracing influencer marketing as a field of advertising, the DSA does end up being relevant for the disclosure of advertising by content creators. This particular obligation builds on already wide-spread practices by platforms, although to an incomplete extent. On the one hand, platforms use misleading labels for advertising, which have shaped a wrong public perception that certain business models (e.g. barter, affiliate marketing) should not be disclosed because the payment is not made in money (see Image 2). On the other hand, in practice, compliance with Article 26(2) has not been easy to assess (see Image 3).

section_path: OVERLAPS WITH OTHER EU REGULATORY REGIMES

paragraphs.text: This should be taken over by the DFA, otherwise an overly fragmented regulatory regime for the already complex creator economy can lead to even more interpretation and enforcement issues.

section_path: OVERLAPS WITH OTHER EU REGULATORY REGIMES

section_path: OVERLAPS WITH OTHER EU REGULATORY REGIMES

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns are referred in terms of interface design

paragraphs.text: So far, dark patterns are referred in terms of interface design, and are generally illustrated in the context of e-commerce. However, social media platforms themselves can be full of interface elements that may manipulate consumers (e.g. the disclosure toggles). Since there already is a risk to overfocus on the role of user interfaces in the

evidence_quotes: offering Virtual Items, or 'gifts' on TikTok LIVE

paragraphs.text: DSA, to the detriment of other manipulative elements such as system design and architecture and even human emotion, the DFA could take a more holistic approach to dark patterns. The DFA should also clarify the boundaries of application shared with the DSA, to avoid regulatory overlap that may lead to risks of over- or underenforcement. For instance, the DFA should clarify the interpretation of complex commercial practices. As can be seen in the case of the Romanian elections, consuming political content happens on a commercial platform with an underlying contractual relation to consumers. As a result, the exposure to political content that attracts a material distortion of the consumers' economic behaviour (e.g. offering Virtual Items, or 'gifts' on TikTok LIVE) should be in itself considered a commercial practice that can be tested for unfairness in the case of manipulation.

According to Article 30 DSA, if content creators use online platforms allowing consumers to conclude distance contracts (e.g. TikTok Shop), and their economic activity is systematic as to be considered traders, they ought to register with the providers of online platforms. This obligation should be harmonized with the more consumer-facing information duties mandated by the Consumer Rights Directive. It is also essential to acknowledge that online platforms allowing consumers to conclude distance contracts include contracts for digital content and services. This means that even without a more 'traditional' e-commerce embedding, platforms that allow for micropayments on live streams are also included in this provision and have relevant obligations. As a result, even streamers who make political content, to the extent that they receive sufficient revenue to be considered traders, should actually register with the platform.

evidence_quotes.quote: endorsement agreements with lump sums for advertising services

paragraphs.text: The operationalization of transparency of commercial and political advertising should be harmonized through platform standards (e.g. Article 11 PAR), especially when it comes to the standardization of publishing ads (or ad campaign details) in a repository (e.g. Article 13 PAR). For content creators (and particularly live streaming) this can be problematic given the revenue incentive structure (e.g. micropayments instead of endorsement agreements with lump sums for advertising services). If supported with micropayments on lives, advertising campaigns might be much more difficult to investigate. This is where a starting point can be a registration obligation for traders (see above).

evidence_quotes.quote: not adding a definition for influencers in the DFA,

paragraphs.text: We suggest not adding a definition for influencers in the DFA, as the business landscape is already changing, the terminology is moving towards 'content creators', and advertising is not the only business
model pursued by creators. That would raise additional legal certainty questions about further defining content creators. Definitions should rather be embedded at Preamble level.

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing
subscriptions

paragraphs.text: "Content creators pursue monetization models on online platforms by building trust and authenticity towards their audiences. Such models include influencer marketing, advertising redistribution programmes, creator funds, micropayments, subscriptions, or selling goods or services (including digital content) directly to consumers."

evidence_quotes.quote: platform ads transparency mandated by the DSA

paragraphs.text: We suggest introducing transparency obligations for platforms to mirror the platform ads transparency mandated by the DSA

evidence_quotes.quote: Content creators who disseminate content professionally or with the intent of monetary gain from third parties should be transparent about the identity of their advertisers and how their content is monetised, for each specific advertisement.

Ads should also distinguish between different business models, and make it clear to consumers that a commercial transaction also includes gifted products or services, affiliate marketing, or other non-pecuniary benefits received by content creators in exchange for their advertising services, including content about product and service reviews.

paragraphs.text: "Platforms should be transparent about what user-generated content is monetised on their services, by whom, and about the basic principles used to allocate resources to content creators. Content creators who disseminate content professionally or with the intent of monetary gain from third parties should be transparent about the identity of their advertisers and how their content is monetised, for each specific advertisement. Ads should also distinguish between different business models, and make it clear to consumers that a commercial transaction also includes gifted products or services, affiliate marketing, or other non-pecuniary benefits received by content creators in exchange for their advertising services, including content about product and service reviews."
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
